items.title: Hinchey classification
items.contentHtml:
Hinchey Classification

  • Hinchey I: small peridiverticular abscess (≤4 cm), contained in the mesentery.
  • Hinchey II: major pelvic or intra-abdominal abscess (>4 cm), not free.
  • Hinchey III: purulent peritonitis generalized due to free perforation.
  • Hinchey IV: fecaloid peritonitis due to free perforation with feces in the cavity.
  • It is based on intraoperative findings; useful for deciding surgical management and prognosis.

items.title: Rope sign in TC
items.contentHtml:
String sign on computed tomography

  • String sign (string sign): intense mural enhancement and thickening of a tubular segment of the sigmoid colon with luminal stenosis, seen on computed tomography with contrast.
  • Indicates acute transmural inflammation, typical of diverticulitis, but nonspecific (can be seen in Crohn's disease).
  • It is associated with Hinchey I or II when there are small adjacent abscesses.
  • It is not pathognomonic; The differential diagnosis includes colorectal cancer, which usually presents asymmetric thickening and heterogeneous enhancement.

items.title: Diverticulitis vs right appendicitis
items.contentHtml:
Diverticulitis vs right appendicitis

  • Right diverticulitis (ascending colon) is rare in the West, but simulates acute appendicitis.
  • Key difference: in right diverticulitis the pain is slower to migrate, with less frequent nausea; in appendicitis, epigastric pain migrates to the right iliac fossa in <24 h.
  • On computed tomography: diverticulitis shows concentric mural thickening with inflamed diverticula; appendicitis has thickening of the appendix (>6 mm) with mural enhancement and target sign.
  • Management of uncomplicated right diverticulitis is medical (antibiotics and diet); Appendicitis requires urgent appendectomy.

items.title: Hinchey criteria I-IV
items.contentHtml:
Hinchey I-IV criteria

  • Hinchey I: small peridiverticular (mesenteric) abscess, ≤4 cm, without peritonitis.
  • Hinchey II: pelvic or intra-abdominal abscess >4 cm, or multiple abscesses, without generalized peritonitis.
  • Hinchey III: peritonitis generalized purulent (inflammatory free fluid without feces).
  • Hinchey IV: fecal peritonitis (feces in cavity) due to free perforation.
  • Grades I-II are managed with antibiotics ± percutaneous drainage; III-IV require urgent surgery (resection with anastomosis or Hartmann).

items.title: Medical vs surgical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Medical vs surgical treatment

  • Uncomplicated diverticulitis (Hinchey I and some small II): outpatient or in-hospital medical treatment with antibiotics, liquid diet and analgesia.
  • Complicated diverticulitis (abscess >4 cm, free perforation, peritonitis, obstruction, fistula): requires urgent surgery or elective.
  • Urgent surgery indicated in Hinchey III and intravenous: laparotomy with sigmoid resection and colostomy (Hartmann) or primary anastomosis if hemodynamic stability.
  • Elective surgery (laparoscopic) is considered after 2 episodes of uncomplicated diverticulitis or 1 complicated episode in young patients (<50 years).

items.title: Indications for urgent surgery
items.contentHtml:
Indications for urgent surgery

  • Generalized peritonitis (Hinchey III/intravenous): signs of diffuse peritoneal irritation, pneumoperitoneum or free fluid with bubbles.
  • Septic shock or hemodynamic instability refractory to resuscitation.
  • Abscess not drainable percutaneously (>5 cm, loculated or inaccessible) with persistent sepsis.
  • Complete intestinal obstruction due to inflammatory stricture or compressive abscess.
  • Colovesical, colovaginal or coloenteric fistula with signs of sepsis or failure of medical treatment.

items.title: Uncomplicated antibiotic therapy
items.contentHtml:
Antibiotic therapy in uncomplicated cases

  • In mild uncomplicated diverticulitis, the current trend is observation without antibiotics in immunocompetent patients without comorbidities (recent clinical trials).
  • If indicated: cover gram-negative and anaerobes. Outpatient regimen: amoxicillin-clavulanic acid 875/125 mg every 12 hours PO × 7 days, or ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours PO + metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours PO.
  • In-hospital: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g intravenous every 6 hours, or ceftriaxone 1 g intravenous every 24 hours + metronidazole 500 mg intravenously every 8 hours.
  • Total duration: 5-7 days; Do not prolong more than 10 days unless complications occur.

items.title: Diet and outpatient management
items.contentHtml:
Diet and outpatient management

  • Mild uncomplicated diverticulitis (fever ≤38°C, leukocytes <15,000, oral tolerance): outpatient management with liquid diet or low residue diet for 2-3 days, then progress to solids as tolerated.
  • Analgesia: paracetamol 500-1000 mg every 6 hours; avoid NSAIDs due to risk of perforation.
  • Hospitalization criteria: fever >38.5°C, leukocytosis >15,000, severe pain, immunosuppression, decompensated comorbidities, inability to tolerate oral administration.
  • Outpatient control: reevaluate in 48-72 hours; If it worsens, perform computed tomography and consider hospitalization.

items.title: Complication: peridiverticular abscess
items.contentHtml:
Complication: peridiverticular abscess

  • Peridiverticular abscess is the most common complication (Hinchey I/II). It is formed by a perforation contained in the mesentery or pelvic space.
  • Diagnosis: computed tomography with contrast shows hypodense collection with peripheral enhancement, gas inside and neighboring mural thickening.
  • Treatment: abscesses ≤4 cm (Hinchey I) usually resolve with intravenous antibiotics; >4 cm require percutaneous drainage guided by computed tomography or ultrasound.
  • If drainage fails or there is persistent sepsis, urgent surgery (resection).

items.title: Free perforation and peritonitis
items.contentHtml:
Free perforation and peritonitis

  • Free perforation = rupture of diverticulum into the peritoneal cavity, resulting in peritonitis (Hinchey III/intravenous).
  • Clinic: generalized abdominal pain, rigidity, guarding, high fever, tachycardia, signs of sepsis.
  • Image: computed tomography shows pneumoperitoneum (subdiaphragmatic free air or bubbles in the mesentery), fluid free, mural thickening.
  • Management: urgent surgery. Peritoneal lavage + sigmoid resection with colostomy (Hartmann) is the gold standard in unstable patients; primary anastomosis ± protective ileostomy in stable cases.

items.title: Recurrence and surgical prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Recurrence and surgical prophylaxis

  • Risk of recurrence after first episode of uncomplicated diverticulitis: 20-30%; after second episode: 50%.
  • Indications for elective surgery (laparoscopic sigmoidecetomy): ≥2 episodes of uncomplicated diverticulitis, or 1 complicated episode (abscess, fistula, obstruction) in patients <50 years or immunocompromised.
  • Prophylaxis: diet rich in fiber (25-35 g/day), avoid NSAIDs and opiates. (they increase the risk of perforation).
  • Mesalazine or probiotics are not recommended to prevent recurrences (insufficient evidence).

items.title: Differential diagnosis with colorectal cancer
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with colorectal cancer

  • Both can present abdominal pain, change in bowel habit and mural thickening on computed tomography.
  • Key differences: in diverticulitis the thickening is concentric and long (>5 cm), with gradual narrowing, adjacent diverticula and homogeneous mural enhancement; in cancer it is asymmetric, short (<5 cm), with heterogeneous enhancement and regional lymphadenopathy.
  • Pericolonic fat sign: in diverticulitis there is intense fatty infiltration (stranding); in cancer it is minimal or absent.
  • Delayed colonoscopy (6-8 weeks post-episode) mandatory to rule out cancer, especially if there is persistent stricture or mass.
